title: IIIF Labyriiinth
text:
Preface

Images have become ubiquitous in the digital age, and IIIF – the International Image Interoperability Framework – re-imagines what you can do with them, and a lot more besides.

“To understand IIIF, you have to look with your hands and think with your eyes.”
Chris Gilman (Digital Curriculum Program Coordinator, UCLA)

IIIF can be quite complex to explain, and often also to use, but some of the principles at play are strikingly simple. You don’t really need to worry too much about what IIIF means to start using it, you just need to know what you can do with it to begin understanding it. But at its most basic level, it means that you can access and, importantly, use a lot of digital content from hundreds of institutions across the world.


Why a labyrinth?
Labyrinths are a great way to inspire and empower you to navigate as you start your own IIIF journey, they are a reflective space ‘in the spirit of good pedagogy’ (Sellers and Moss 2016, 9).
The IIIF Labyriiinth is an open educational resource for introducing and teaching IIIF Literacy, Methods and Tools. It has been constructed by Andy Corrigan as part of his CDH Methods Fellowship 2025/6.
Below is an example of a labyrinth






Sellers, Jan, and Bernard Moss. 2016. Learning with the Labyrinth : Creating Reflective Space in Higher Education / Edited by Jan Sellers, Bernard Moss. Palgrave Teaching and Learning. Basingstoke, Hampshire: Palgrave Macmillan.

title: 1  Creating Reflective Space
text:
1.1 Learning Objective
Create virtual storage spaces in which to save your work, and a journal in order to be able to reflect on and share your learning journey in the IIIF Labyriiinth.

title: 1  Creating Reflective Space
text:
NoteKey Tools and Concepts





Exhibit, IIIF Manifest, Mixtape, IIIF Workbench

title: 1  Creating Reflective Space
text:
1.2 Summary
Before you enter the IIIF Labyriiinth, we need to make sure you have prepared for your journey.
We will use a variety interactive reflective spaces on our journey, but first you are going to create some to stash your stuuff, and a digital journal in which you can record and reflect on what you learn.

title: 1  Creating Reflective Space
text:
1.3 Workbench
First thing’s first, if you haven’t already, sign-up for a GitHub account - it’s free and the IIIF Workbench will be able to store your IIIF files there so you can make use of them.
Next, create your IIIF Workbench.






TipExercise 1





Follow these steps:
1. Open the IIIF Workbench in a new browser tab.
2. Login using your GutHub account.
3. Click the ‘Authorize’ button.
4. Click the ‘Create Project’ button.
5. Give your project a unique name.
6. You’ll come back and use this on jounrey in the IIIF Labyriiinth, so you might want to keep the tab open.

title: 1  Creating Reflective Space
text:
1.4 MixTape
Next, you might want to prepare a IIIF Mixtape for you jounrey. You can use this stash your favourite IIIF resources for later.






TipExercise 2





Follow these steps:
1. In a new browser tab, open the IIIF MixTape.
2. Click the ‘New Collection’ button.
3. Enter a name for your Mixtape, enter your email address and tick the T’s&C’s.
4. Click the ‘Create Collection’ button.
5. You will shortly recieve an email with a link to your very own IIIF Mixtape - Keep it safe!

title: 1  Creating Reflective Space
text:
1.5 Journal
The last thing you need to prepare is your Journal. To create this, you are going to use a tool called Exhibit.






TipExercise 3





Follow these steps:
1. In a new browser tab, go to: Exhibit.so.
2. Click the ‘Create an Exhibit ->’ button.
3. Fill in the form using these options:
- Template: Select ‘Slides’
- Title: [Your Name]‘s IIIF Labyrinth Journal
- Author: [Your Name]
- Description:
- Rights (optional):
- Access: Select ’Public’ -> You can password protect if you’d rather, but make sure you remember the password!
- Allow Duplication: You can untick this if you want.
- I’m not a robot: Tick
- Ts&Cs: Tick
4. Click ‘Create Exhibit’.



Next, find a cover image!






TipExercise 4





Follow these steps:
1. In another browser tab, navigate to this Mixtape of IIIF Examples.
2. Pick one of the items that you think looks interesting, and click on the ‘IIIF’ logo for it.
3. A page of ‘code’ will open. This is JSON, it might look scary but you only need the URL! Go ahead and ‘copy’ the URL.
4. Go back to your ‘Exhibit’ tab, and click ‘+ Add Item’ button
5. Paste the URL into the ‘Import’ box in the ‘+ Add Item’ dialogue window and click ‘Import’.
6. You should see the item appear in a zoomable viewer on the right hand side of the screen. Find the image you want to use using the thumbnails. 7. On the left-hand side of the screen, click the ‘+’ button directly under your title/description box.
8. Type: ‘This is my front cover image.’ -> if you like, you can also add a brief sentence explaining what drew you to this image.
9. Click the ‘tick’ button.



Next, you need to ‘Share’ your journal: * Click the ‘Share’ button in the bottom left of the screen. * In the window you should see two ‘Copy’ buttons. The top one also has a drop-down option that can be set to ‘edit’ OR ‘view’. * You need to copy both of these URLs and save them somewhere you can easily access again. You could send yourself an email containing the links. For this lesson we want to share our journals, so navigate another browser tab to this GoogleDoc. * Once you have saved your links somewhere, you can close (‘X’) the ‘Share’ window.






ImportantImportant



Keep this ‘Exhibit’ page open in a browser tab whilst we undertake the other exercises, and we will return to it and add more content later.


You can now take your first step and enter the IIIF Labyrinth…

title: 2  The object of Inquiry
text: 2.1 Learning Objective

title: 2  The object of Inquiry
text:
Annotate a IIIF Resource in order to understand that they can comprise of images and text.

Evaluate the concept of interoperability in order to understand what IIIF is.







NoteKey Tools and Concepts





IIIF Manifest, Annotation

title: 2  The object of Inquiry
text:
2.2 Summary
It’s tricky to understand IIIF is until you have a go. This step will allow you to develop and reflect on what IIIF might mean.

title: 2  The object of Inquiry
text:
2.3 What Does IIIF Stand For?
First thing’s first. IIIF is pronounced “tripple-eye-eff”.
The next thing you need to know is what the IIIF ‘icon’/logo looks like:

IIIF is an acronym for the International Image Interoperability Framework, but that’s probably the least important thing to know.






NoteHint





To emphasise this, I often write it ‘IIIF (International Image Interoperability Framework)’ rather than ‘International Image Interoperability Framework (IIIF)’.



Let’s chat about what really IIIF is:

Community - one of the foremost things. IIIF is a huge global community of people and institutions working openly together.

High quality digital objects of inquiry. One of the biggest benefits of digitisation is that it unlocks cultural heritage from museums and libraries.

API’s (Application Programming Interfaces) … Oh no, not another acronym! Dont panic, just think of these as ‘standards’ - ways for computers to talk to each other.

Tools and methods that enable inquiry. IIIF is a completely different way to experience the digital landscape that begins to embody it.

title: 2  The object of Inquiry
section: 2.4 The Broad and Narrow IIIF Way
text:
2.4 The Broad and Narrow IIIF Way
IIIF isn’t very tangible is it?
No. But everyone perceives things differently because we all see and experience different small parts of it. (Popple, 2009, Encyclopaedia of Perception) So something complex like IIIF might be best understood through collective reflection on what we experience.
‘The Broad and the Narrow Way’ is a Victorian poster that accompanied a lecture that taught a somewhat crude world view that human nature tended to be bad, and as a result the path to Heaven in the picture is so difficult and narrow that there are few people are travelling it.

title: 2  The object of Inquiry
text:
2.5 Exercise
We’re going to use a real-time collaborative viewing tool called Liiive.
Try accessing this Liiive room: - The Broad and Narrow Way
Spend a few minutes just having a really good look at what’s in the image and thinking about the various things its comprised of.
Can you find?: - A place on the path to damnation that isn’t necisarily a bad place? - The names of one of the people who had a role in creating it? - A steam train? - A lion? - A route through which you might change paths?
What else can you see?
In the top-right, you’ll see different shapes of annotation tools: - Try adding different types of annotation to the things you’ve spotted - a “rectangle”/“polygon”, an “eliptical”, or a “path”, and include a comment with what you’ve noticed. - You might see someone else has beaten you too it - have a go at replying to an annotation that someone else has added.
In the top left you will find an option to export annotations. You can save them as an Annotation List or as a whole new ‘Manifest’. If you want, you can download these and save them in your Workbench. Or you can create a visual record of one of your annotations by taking a sscreenshot and uploading that image to your Workbench.
What are you looking at, is it image/text/data/code? - If you want, you could add this resource to your journal using the following URL: https://cudl.lib.cam.ac.uk//iiif/PR-BROADSIDES-AA-00008-00023.json
How do you feel about code?

title: 3  Seeing is Believing
text: 3.1 Learning Objective

title: 3  Seeing is Believing
text:
Understand the Image API in order to know what a IIIF Image is.

Apply this knowledge in order to demonstrate you can retrieve a IIIF Image.







NoteKey Tools and Concepts





Image API,

title: 3  Seeing is Believing
text:
3.2 Summary
Ok, so IIIF looks pretty useful - This step will allow you to start making sense of it.
You are going to learn about the IIIF Image API, which is how to access and use an image made available through IIIF.

title: 3  Seeing is Believing
text:
3.3 Theory
You’ve probably heard a URL. This will turn your world upside down, or at least the way you see images on the internet.
Think of an image as a map, and a URL as some co-ordinates on that map and instructions of how to find it.
This is an image of a page from a 1580 CE copy of a manuscript on Jain cosmology that was composed in the Prakrit language in the 14th century. Let’s say the digital image measures 1000 pixels wide by 500 pixels high.



An image of a page from a manuscript on Jain cosmology.


On the page, there is a diagram of the ‘Two and a Half Continents’ (Aḍhāīdvīpa), a representation of the Jain understanding of the earthly realm. It shows lots of things you might expect to see on a map - oceans, continents, rivers and mountains.
If I just want a square region showing only this diagram, I can use IIIF to get just the section I need by thinking of the pixels as co-ordinates.



An image of a page from a manuscript on Jain cosmology.


The top-left corner of the image is always (0,0) and the region is defined using the top-left (x,y) co-ordinate followed by the width and height of the region you want. So in this example, the co-ordinates for the region I want are (525,125,375,375).
But that’s not the only thing we can do. It’s also possible to use the IIIF Image API to resize and rotate the image, and request different qualities or formats. These can be numerical or sometimes use words like ‘full’ or ‘default’.
This image shows the original region, a reduction to 80%, an 80% reduction rotated 90°, and an 80% reduction rotated 180° in greyscale.



An image of a page from a manuscript on Jain cosmology.


So in this case, you really can turn the world upside down! Or make it greyscale if don’t want a colourful world…
The full URI syntax template looks like this:

{scheme}://{server}{/prefix}/{identifier}/{region}/{size}/{rotation}/{quality}.{format}

title: 3  Seeing is Believing
text:
3.4 Example






CautionTo Do in Future





Build interactive OJS Viewer.

title: 3  Seeing is Believing
text:
3.5 Exercise

Find an image you want to use.

In a new tab, go to this Mixtape of IIIF Examples.
Choose one that looks interesting.
Click to the ‘Crop’ icon underneath the resource you’ve chosen.

A new window will open that shows all the available images/pages as thumbnails.
Find one you want to use and click on it.
In the box immediately above the thumbnails you will see a URL and a ‘copy’ button.
Click the ‘copy’ button, open a new web-browser tab and paste it into the address/URL bar and press return/enter.
You should see your image load.

Take a look at the URL and you’ll notice some familiar syntax.
Play with some of these values in the URL, hit enter, and see what happens.
Try changing the following:

Find a region you like
Change the size
Rotate the image
Make it grey or bitonal

Once you are happy with the way your image looks, record your results for your journal by taking a screenshot of you web-browser tab, making sure to include the image and its URL. Make ure you remember where your screenshot is saved, and you can uplaod this to your Workbench.







TipTop Tip!





When you are adding images to your own website (eg Drupal/Wordpress), you could use the IIIF Image API to add the image/crop you want by using these URLs instead of cropping and uploading a new image.
However… there’s also a better way - you could embed a zooming IIIF viewer like Universal Viewer to show ANY IIIF resource in all its glory.
There’s a few hints and wrinkles when doing this - ie wathc out for ‘content state’ - regions in the embed code that can help/hinder. AND sometime the canvase ID is one out if the sequence starts with 0 instead of 1.

title: 4  Code Breaking
text:
4.1 Learning Objective
Analyse a IIIF slide-puzzle in order to gain confidence working with “code”.

title: 4  Code Breaking
text:
NoteKey Tools and Concepts





Image API, Puzzle, Code

title: 4  Code Breaking
text:
4.2 Summary
So IIIF is looking a bit techie and a lot like code. This step will help you experience how dealing with code isn’t as scary as it first looks.

title: 4  Code Breaking
4  Code Breaking
text:
4.4 Exercise
Create your own puzzle using this tool: https://www.tripleyefish.com/puzzle/builder.html
Use the Mixtape of IIIF Examples to find another image you want to use and the ‘Cropping’ tool to pick a region and get your IIIF Image URL.
[Or you can just use the same one as above: https://images.lib.cam.ac.uk/iiif/MS-ADD-00864-000-00140.jp2/1339,201,4066,3678/1200,/0/default.jpg]
You can adjust the number of colllums and tile/puzzle widths.
You can switch between looking at the code, and seeing the image tiles.
How do you feel about code now? Take a screenshot and save it for your journal by uploading it to your IIIF Workbench!

title: 6  Present and Correct
text: 6.1 Learning Objective

title: 6  Present and Correct
text:
Understand the Presentation API in order to know how a digital representation of an object is constructed/structured.

Apply this knowledge in order to discover new resources.







NoteKey Tools and Concepts





Exhibit, IIIF Manifest

title: 6  Present and Correct
text:
6.2 Summary
So you’ve gotten to grips with the IIIF Image API. Well Done! But how are individual images combined with metadata in order to present complete digital objects?

title: 6  Present and Correct
text:
6.3 Theory
What is a IIIF Manifest and what are its components?

Blue = Image data - using the Image API.
Red = The structure: Title/metadata/sequence - using the Presentation API.







flowchart TD
  A[Manifest] --> B[Sequence]
  B --> C{Canvas}
  C --> D(Content A One)
  C --> E(Content B Two)






How do I find IIIF Manifests? Look for the logo, or their sometimes hidden in ‘use’ or ‘share’ options. It’s tricky because everyone does it slightly differentl, and often with good reason. So learning how to find them is a skill.
The IIIF Community have prepared Guides to finding IIIF resources

title: 6  Present and Correct
text:
6.4 Exercise
Pick one of the Guides to finding IIIF resources and see if you can find a manifest for a IIIF resource.
Edit a manifest: Using the Digirati Manifest Editor.

title: 8  Drawing Conclusions

8.1 Learning Objective
Understand the limitiatons of automation in order to be able to demonstrate your digital literacy.

title: 8  Drawing Conclusions

NoteKey Tools and Concepts





Exhibit, IIIF Manifest

title: 8  Drawing Conclusions
text:
8.2 Summary
You can input IIIF manifests into lots of different tools, including ones that can apply machine learning and computer vision (yes - AI!).

title: 8  Drawing Conclusions
text:
8.3 Exercise
Have a go at using a IIIF resource to undertake some automated analysis.
Using a IIIF Illustration Detector.

Enter this manifest: https://cudl.lib.cam.ac.uk//iiif/PR-FH-00910-00083-00098

Click ‘Load Manifest’.

Set the ‘Confidence Threshold’ to 85%.

Tick ‘Show only illustrated pages’, and Click ‘Classify’.

Once it’s finished, click ‘View All Pages’ and you will be able to look through just the pages with illustrations on. Handy!

Let’s try that again with a different resource…
This time, enter this manifest: https://cudl.lib.cam.ac.uk//iiif/MS-OR-00770
What do you notice?
Why do you think this is?
Add the two manifests to you journal and include your answers.
This exercise used an easy to use tool, but you can apply all sorts of AI models to do different things - even transcribe handwriting! There’s a tool for that: Transkribus.
But to really get into AI models, you might be better off learning to code using Python/R.

title: Appendix A — About the IIIF Labyriiinth
text:
A.1 Intro
What’s different about the IIIF Labyriiinth?
It has been designed as a journey that begins with the creation of a self-reflective digital space that the learner can build their experience as they learn more about IIF.
By starting with the creation of this space, the learner has already begun using IIIF before they develop an understanding of what IIIF it is.
in line with the concepts of labyrinth -[main principles…].

title: Appendix A — About the IIIF Labyriiinth
text:
A.2 Principles

Grounded in pedagogical theory - The IIIF Labyriiinth has been developed using theoretical approaches like Kolb’s Learning Cycle [CITE!] and Wiggins and McTigue’s Backward Design [CITE!].
Designed to compliment the Cambridge Information Literacy Framework

title: Appendix A — About the IIIF Labyriiinth
text:
A.3 Acknowledgements

The IIIF Training resources developed by developed by Glen Robson for the IIIF Consortium
DISKAH - Digital Skills in Arts and Humanities Network, particularly they’re workshop on Accessing and Interacting with Multidimensional Media.
The modules [LINK?] developed by Chris Gilman (UCLA), Chien-Ling Liu Zeleny (UCLA), and Ben Johnston (Princeton)
